---
title: "Microgrid Setup Workflow"
subtitle: "Econ-IoT MEMS Architecture & Lifecycle"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
    preview-links: auto
    chalkboard: true
---

# Introduction to Econ MEMS

## Overview

The Econ-IoT system provides a robust, latency-aware **Microgrid Energy Management System (MEMS)** designed to govern isolated operations like the Kisengo mining project.

*   **Focus**: Physical safety, operational clarity, reproducibility.
*   **Methodology**: Highly reproducible logic and architecture.

## System Lifecycle

Our engineering workflow focuses strictly on design, simulation, and verification before physical deployment.

1.  **State Machine Definition**: Core logic via XState v5.
2.  **Formal Verification**: Simulated path traversal mapping.
3.  **Simulation & Testing**: `pvlib` & `mosaik` CHIL.
4.  **EMS Implementation**: Edge deployment with hardware.

---

# 1. State Machine Definition

## What is IEEE 2030.7?

> *IEEE Standard for the Specification of Microgrid Controllers*

The **IEEE 2030.7-2017** standard formally defines the core functional blocks of a microgrid controller. It establishes how a cluster of distributed energy resources (DERs) and loads must transition uniformly between logical states.

*   Ensures reliable grid connection and islanding sequence.
*   Standardizes the interoperability of microgrid components.

## XState modeling

To securely map and implement the IEEE 2030.7 standard, we utilize **XState v5**.

*   By mathematically enforcing states, we eliminate undefined behavior (e.g., an "off" generator trying to sync).
*   **Why not raw SCXML?** While SCXML is our end-goal format, manually authoring raw SCXML is notoriously error-prone and lacks modern tooling support. XState provides robust TypeScript integration, visual editors, and strict validation, while easily compiling *down* to strict SCXML for the controller.

## The MEMS Statechart

```{mermaid}
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    
    INITIALIZE: INITIALIZE
    SS1: SS1_GRID_CONNECTED
    T1: T1_PLANNED_ISLAND
    T2: T2_UNPLANNED_ISLAND
    SS2: SS2_STABLE_ISLAND
    T3: T3_RECONNECTION

    INITIALIZE --> SS1 : GRID_AVAILABLE

    SS1 --> T1 : PLANNED_COMMAND
    SS1 --> T2 : GRID_FAULT

    T1 --> SS2 : TRANSFER_COMPLETE
    T1 --> SS1 : TRANSFER_FAILED

    T2 --> SS2 : FREQUENCY_STABLE
    T2 --> INITIALIZE : ISLAND_FAILED

    SS2 --> T3 : RECONNECT_COMMAND
    SS2 --> T3 : GRID_RESTORED
    SS2 --> T2 : ISLAND_FAULT

    T3 --> SS1 : SYNC_COMPLETE
    T3 --> SS2 : SYNC_FAILED
```

<div class="notes">
Here you can observe the actual six operational states corresponding exactly to the IEEE documentation, strictly bounding when and how our microgrid transitions to islanded scenarios.
</div>

---

# 2. Formal Verification

## Assuring Transition Integrity

Once the parameter boundaries and logic guards are defined in XState, the structure must be mathematically proven before risking physical hardware.

## Formal Verification Contenders

We are actively evaluating the following model checkers to guarantee transition safety:

*   **TLA+**: Industry standard for distributed systems. Excellent at proving liveness and safety properties, but has a steep learning curve.
*   **Spin (Promela)**: Highly optimized for software verification and multi-threaded race conditions.
*   **UPPAAL**: Specialized in real-time systems. Native integration with timed automata makes it a strong contender for analyzing precise electrical transient windows.

*(Placeholder: Image showing SCXML diagram or formal verification interface)*
![](verification_placeholder.png)

---

# 3. Simulation & Testing

## Synthesizing Real-World Conditions

Before any code touches a physical controller, the verified logic is evaluated dynamically using **Digital Twin** conditions mimicking target microgrids.

*   **Solar Profile**: We utilize `pvlib` to approximate AC solar generation curves deterministically.
    *   *Alternatives*: NREL SAM or OpenDSS. `pvlib` was chosen for its Python-native integration and lightweight profile generation.
*   **Load Profile**: We stochasticially simulate heavy industrial mining loads.

## Load Profile Anomalies

Our simulator generates boundary anomalies like induction motor start-up transients.

```{mermaid}
xychart-beta
    title "Rock Crusher Inrush Profile (Simulated)"
    x-axis [0s, 1s, 2s, 3s, 4s, 5s]
    y-axis "Draw (kW)" 0 --> 1500
    line [1500, 1000, 600, 400, 300, 250]
```

These extreme start-up currents are accurately reflected in our `load_profile.csv` generation to test the control limits of the battery integration.

## Mosaik Co-Simulation (CHIL)

What is **mosaik**?

Mosaik is a flexible co-simulation framework that allows independent simulators to interact within a synchronized time loop. It provides the Controller Hardware-in-the-Loop (CHIL) orchestration.

## The Role of Mosaik vs Commercial HIL

Mosaik orchestrates a pure software-in-the-loop physics environment.

* **Does it replace commercial options?** No. While mosaik perfectly validates the state transitions and protocol bridging (software integration), final sign-off still requires dedicated hyper-realtime appliances.
* **Eventual HIL target**: Commercial offerings like **Typhoon HIL** or OPAL-RT will eventually be used to test exact sub-millisecond inverter hardware responses before Kisengo deployment.

## Modbus TCP Bridging

Mosaik calculates physical reality, but the controller expects to speak to vendor hardware.

*   The orchestrator uses an async **Modbus TCP** bridge.
*   It dynamically proxies the physical outputs (Voltage, SoC) down to standard holding registers.
*   **Result**: The logic tests against registers mapped accurately to proprietary **Deep Sea Electronics (DSE)** and **Sungrow** protocols.

*(Placeholder: Architecture diagram of mosaik connecting out to Modbus clients)*
![](mosaik_bridge_placeholder.png)

---

# 4. EMS Implementation

## Deploying to Edge Hardware

Following successful simulation against the CHIL Modbus environment, the validated data contracts and schemas are deployed.

1.  **Transport**: Edge devices ingest sensor states and publish to MQTT.
2.  **Streaming**: Redpanda consumes the topics; `FastStream` handles Python pydantic validation.
3.  **Storage**: Validated telemetry routes cleanly to `TimescaleDB`.

## The Nix Guarantee

A core tenet of our deployment is environment reproducibility via **Nix**.

* By containerizing our development environments (flakes) and process orchestration (process-compose), we eliminate "works on my machine" drift.
* **Summary**: Containerized testing (Nix) + verified states (XState) ensures:
> *"The on-site physical controller node acts exactly like the testbed mock."*

---

# 5. Remote Operations (DRC)

## Challenge: The Kisengo Site

Operating in the Democratic Republic of the Congo presents substantial logistical hurdles. Flying engineers to address software bugs is prohibitively expensive and slow.

We require a secure, reliable Software-Defined Wide Area Network (SD-WAN) to manage the edge controllers.

## SD-WAN Contenders

*   **ZeroTier**: Battle-tested and extremely easy to set up. However, relies heavily on proprietary controller infrastructure.
*   **Tailscale**: Built on WireGuard, incredible usability. However, it requires reliance on their centralized coordination servers.
*   **EasyTier**: A decentralized, open-source VPN solution. It builds peer-to-peer networks without requiring a central topology.

## Why EasyTier?

We are currently leaning towards **EasyTier** for the Kisengo implementation.

1.  **Fully OSS**: No vendor lock-in or licensing bottlenecks.
2.  **Decentralized**: Operates independently even if an external coordinating node goes down, crucial for unreliable African ISPs.
3.  **Bridge functionality**: Easily bridges the isolated PLC subnets out to the secure engineering network securely.

## EasyTier Architecture

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    subgraph HQ [Engineering HQ]
        Eng[Engineer Laptop]
    end

    subgraph Cloud [Public Cloud]
        PublicNode[EasyTier Public Relay Node]
    end

    subgraph Kisengo [Kisengo Site (DRC)]
        Edge[Edge Controller]
        subgraph PLC_Subnet [Microgrid OT Network]
            DSE[Diesel Controller]
            BESS[BESS Inverter]
        end
        Edge -->|Modbus TCP| DSE
        Edge -->|Modbus TCP| BESS
    end

    Eng <-->|EasyTier P2P / Relay| Edge
    Eng -.->|Fallback| PublicNode
    Edge -.->|Fallback| PublicNode
```

<div class="notes">
With EasyTier, the engineer can remotely tunnel directly securely to the Edge Controller, which acting as a proxy, allows diagnostic access directly to the physical modbus registers without exposing the industrial control layer to the public internet.
</div>
